does: This paper describes OpenAI's operational monitoring system for detecting misalignment in coding agents deployed internally. The approach uses chain-of-thought monitoring—analyzing the intermediate reasoning steps and outputs of code-generating AI systems—to identify behavioral anomalies and potential safety violations. The authors present concrete methods for monitoring real production deployments of coding agents, document specific misalignment risks they detected, and show how their monitoring framework enabled them to strengthen safety measures and prevent harmful behaviors before they reached users.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on AI safety monitoring and interpretability research that seeks to understand and supervise model behavior in deployment. It extends earlier research on chain-of-thought explanations and mechanistic interpretability by applying these techniques to the practical problem of detecting misalignment in live systems. The paper responds to the challenge of scale that deployment safety research increasingly faces—moving beyond isolated evaluations to continuous monitoring of real-world agent behavior. It also contributes to the broader effort within the safety community to develop methods for post-deployment oversight that complement pre-deployment testing and alignment training.
enables: This work establishes a framework and methodology for continuous misalignment detection that other organizations can adapt for monitoring their own deployed agents. By demonstrating practical monitoring techniques on coding systems, it points toward more general approaches for safety oversight in complex autonomous agents. The paper enables future research into automated intervention strategies that respond to detected misalignment in real-time, potentially moving toward closed-loop safety systems. It also suggests future directions for scaling monitoring to more capable agents, integrating human judgment with automated detection, and using monitoring signals to inform alignment research at earlier development stages.
